english: Grain bowl with chicken and 4 toppings

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for 'chicken grain bowl with 4 toppings'; mapped to the closest grain bowl food product class. Exact multi-topping customisable bowl is not represented in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Composite dish — cereal-based dish with meat and vegetables
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for customisable grain bowls with 4 toppings; A0CKP (composite cereal-based dish with meat and vegetables) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to a customisable grain bowl food product; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: mixed grains (brown rice, quinoa, or similar whole grains)
choice of four vegetable or protein toppings
allergens: gluten (possible — grain base may include wheat-containing grains or sauce)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g (one bowl), reflecting a typical restaurant portion of mixed grains, grilled chicken, four vegetable toppings, and sauce as served at health-casual grain bowl outlets in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Grain Bowl (4 Toppings) — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: All per-100g nutrient values are taken directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for 'Chicken Grain Bowl (4 Toppings)' (cr_id F08010004) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite chicken-and-grain dish data (SR Legacy #21421 and #20041) because HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this entry; this is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — mixed grain and legume dishes; analogous estimate based on mixed grain bowl with chicken and vegetables
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a customisable chicken grain bowl with 4 toppings. GI of 48 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for mixed-grain dishes (brown rice + quinoa blend GI ~45–52, mean 48) combined with the protein and fibre content of this dish (protein 8.4 g/100g, fibre 2.26 g/100g) which further attenuate glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as: GI × net available carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 48 × (9.5 g/100g - 2.26 g/100g) × 350 g serving / 100 = 48 × 7.24 g/100g × 3.5 / 100 = 48 × 25.34 / 100 ≈ 12.16, rounded to 12.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 252 mg/100g (882 mg per 350g serving) approaches the 1000 mg/meal threshold for hypertension management; sauce choice is the primary sodium driver.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request sauce on the side and choose vinaigrette or olive oil over teriyaki or soy-based sauces, as this can reduce per-serving sodium by an estimated 150–300 mg; the potassium contribution from four vegetable toppings (~230 mg/100g) partially offsets sodium load and supports blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low estimated GI (~48) combined with dietary fibre (2.26 g/100g) and high protein (8.39 g/100g) attenuates postprandial glucose response; net available carbohydrate is 7.24 g/100g.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes can include this dish as part of a balanced meal plan; selecting brown rice or a quinoa-dominant grain base, choosing low-sugar sauces, and monitoring total portion size (≤350g) will help maintain postprandial blood glucose within target range.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 252 mg/100g is the primary cardiovascular concern; fat profile is moderate (4.76 g/100g total fat, estimated saturated fat ~1.2 g/100g) and the dish provides dietary fibre (2.26 g/100g) which supports LDL reduction.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should choose olive oil or vinaigrette dressing to maximise MUFA intake, request sauce on the side to limit sodium, and select antioxidant-rich toppings such as broccoli, spinach, or edamame to support endothelial health; the fibre content from whole grains and four vegetable toppings contributes to LDL cholesterol reduction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Four vegetable toppings provide antioxidants (estimated vitamin C ~6 mg/100g) and dietary fibre (2.26 g/100g) from whole-food sources; whole grains and lean chicken support an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should select anti-inflammatory toppings such as broccoli, spinach, edamame, or carrot and request olive oil-based dressing to add MUFA anti-inflammatory benefit; nightshade toppings (tomato, bell pepper) should be avoided if the individual is sensitive to nightshades.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Chicken Grain Bowl (4 Toppings) is a contemporary health-casual dish popular in Singapore, consisting of grilled or roasted chicken served over a base of mixed whole grains — typically a blend of brown rice, quinoa, and/or other ancient grains — topped with a choice of four vegetable or protein toppings and a sauce. Common toppings include broccoli, edamame, spinach, carrot, corn, roasted sweet potato, and hard-boiled egg; common sauces include vinaigrette, teriyaki, tahini, and sweet chilli. The dish is assembled rather than cooked as a single unit, giving it a modular, customisable character. Nutritionally, it provides 115 kcal per 100g with a balanced macronutrient profile: protein 8.39 g, fat 4.76 g, and carbohydrate 9.5 g per 100g. Dietary fibre at 2.26 g/100g and the whole-grain base contribute to a low estimated glycaemic index (~48), making it suitable for blood glucose management. Sodium at 252 mg/100g is the primary clinical concern and is largely driven by the sauce component. The four-topping variant provides marginally more fibre, potassium, and antioxidants than the three-topping version, improving its cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory profile. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore outlets, low in calories, and suitable for weight management, Type 2 Diabetes, and general healthy eating. It is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner meal and is widely available at health-casual grain bowl chains across Singapore.
common_user_queries: Calories in chicken grain bowl Singapore
Is grain bowl good for diabetes?
Best toppings for grain bowl diabetes

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) are sourced directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for this exact dish (cr_id F08010004) with a reported confidence of 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy composite data and is clearly documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and is documented as such. Overall confidence is high for the HPB-sourced macros and medium for the estimated GI and saturated fat.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.2 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite chicken-and-grain dish entries (#21421 grilled chicken breast, #20041 brown rice cooked) because HPB Foodo does not report saturated fat separately for this entry. This value should be updated when HPB publishes a full fatty acid profile. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid, micronutrient, and moisture data for this customisable composite dish are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a matched entry as of 2026-04-16. 3. Potassium (230 mg/100g) and vitamin C (6 mg/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for analogous chicken-and-vegetable grain dishes and are used in clinical reasoning; these should be confirmed against HPB when data become available. 4. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (mixed grain dishes, brown rice + quinoa blend); no direct GI measurement exists for this specific customisable dish. Glycaemic load per 350g serving ≈ 12.1 (net available carbohydrate basis). 5. Sauce composition is variable and is the primary driver of sodium variability; all sodium-related clinical guidance assumes a moderate sauce portion. 6. The dish is tagged halal based on the input record dietary_tags; this should be verified against the specific outlet's halal certification.
